docs.title: Wetlands
docs.text:
Wetlands runs Python functions, scripts, and commands inside isolated Pixi or Conda-compatible environments from a normal Python process.

Wetlands 2 is organized around explicit ownership:

  • WetlandsSession owns workers in the current Python process.
  • WetlandsService owns persistent service state.
  • connect() returns a client handle to that service.
  • EnvSpec describes environment dependencies.

The base install keeps terminal UI dependencies optional. Install the interactive monitor only when needed:

pip install "wetlands[tui]"

docs.title: Minimal Session
docs.text:
from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsSession

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy==2.2.4"]), workers=1)
    result = env.call("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([1, 2, 3],))
    print(result)

docs.title: Persistent Service
docs.text:
from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsService, connect

WetlandsService.ensure_running(root="wetlands")
wet = connect(root="wetlands")

env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy==2.2.4"]), workers=2)
tasks = env.submit_many("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", [[1, 2], [3, 4]])
print([task.wait().result for task in tasks])

wet.close()

The current service transport is in-process only. A client can reconnect only while the service endpoint is registered in the same Python process. Task and process records are in-memory and are lost when the service stops.

docs.title: Main APIs
docs.text:
• env.call() for blocking function calls.
  • env.submit() for one asynchronous task.
  • env.map() for mapped results.
  • env.submit_many() for mapped task handles.
  • env.module() for a dynamic blocking module proxy in session-owned environments.
  • env.run_script() for complete Python scripts.
  • env.run() for blocking shell commands.
  • env.spawn() for long-running commands with a process proxy.

docs.title: Current Boundaries
docs.text:
Wetlands 2 currently has:

  • local, trusted, in-process service transport;
  • in-memory task and process state;
  • an optional trusted-local Textual monitor with selected debug attach and non-force stop confirmation;
  • no durable task or process store;
  • no task log stream;
  • no real daemon log stream.

The docs name these boundaries where they matter so applications can choose the right lifecycle model today.

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
Wetlands records debug metadata for workers so an IDE can attach to code running inside an isolated environment.

Create a debug-enabled session:

from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsSession

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands", debug=True) as wet:
    env = wet.env("debug-demo", EnvSpec(pip=[]), workers=1)
    env.call("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([1, 2, 3],))

Or start a debug-enabled service:

from wetlands import WetlandsService

WetlandsService.ensure_running(root="wetlands", debug=True)

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
List debuggable workers:

wetlands debug list --root wetlands

The command prints JSON debug target snapshots. It is read-only: it reads debug_ports.json under the selected root and does not scan processes, start environments, or prune metadata. The legacy wetlands list --root wetlands alias now uses the same typed debug target output.

Debug metadata registration is root-local and atomically replaces root/debug_ports.json. If a metadata write is interrupted or fails, the previous complete file remains in place. Temporary atomic-write files are ignored by debug listing. Malformed debug_ports.json makes debug targets unavailable and is reported by discovery; Wetlands does not repair it automatically.

Create and open an IDE attach configuration:

wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --ide vscode
wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --ide pycharm

debug attach reads the worker debug metadata under the requested Wetlands root and writes an IDE configuration with the matching local path mapping. It does not switch to a different root discovered from running processes.

docs.title: Multiple Workers
docs.text:
An environment can have multiple worker processes. Each worker has its own debug port. When a worker is replaced, debug metadata is refreshed for the new process. If multiple live workers are available, attach requires an explicit target selector:

wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --worker debug-demo:1
wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --worker 1
wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --pid 12345
wetlands debug attach debug-demo --root wetlands --sources . --target debug-demo:1

If a debugger cannot attach, run wetlands debug list --root wetlands and verify that the environment has live workers and recorded ports.

Read-only discovery exposes capability reasons for monitor interfaces. Attach is disabled with stable reasons such as debug-metadata-malformed, debug-target-unavailable, debug-target-stale, debug-port-unavailable, or module-executor-path-unavailable.

The optional Textual monitor can launch the same attach flow for the selected eligible debug-target row:

pip install "wetlands[tui]"
wetlands monitor --root wetlands --tui --sources . --ide vscode

If an environment has multiple eligible workers, select the concrete debug target row before attaching. The environment row itself is not enough to disambiguate. Real IDE attach remains a manual smoke test: verify that VS Code or PyCharm opens the generated remote attach configuration and connects to the selected worker PID/port.

docs.title: Current Limits
docs.text: Debug metadata describes live local worker processes. It is not a durable service log or task log. If the service is using the current in-process transport and no worker endpoint is registered in the current process, connect() and debug operations fail clearly.

docs.title: Specifying Dependencies
docs.text:
EnvSpec is the Wetlands 2 public dependency model.

from wetlands import EnvSpec, PackageSpec

spec = EnvSpec(
    python="3.12",
    conda=[
        PackageSpec("numpy", "==2.2.4"),
        PackageSpec("cellpose", "==3.1.0"),
    ],
    pip=["imageio"],
    channels=["conda-forge"],
)

PackageSpec is useful when a dependency needs metadata such as platform constraints, channel, optional status, or no-dependency installation.

docs.title: Spec Files
docs.text:
Wetlands can build an EnvSpec from common project files:

EnvSpec.from_file("requirements.txt")
EnvSpec.from_file("environment.yml")
EnvSpec.from_file("pyproject.toml", optional_dependencies=["analysis"])
EnvSpec.from_file("pixi.toml", environment="default")

Spec files are parsed by the caller. In service mode, the client sends normalized spec data to the service rather than asking the service to read an arbitrary local client path.

docs.title: Fingerprints
docs.text:
Wetlands records a fingerprint of the normalized spec. Source metadata, such as the file path and source hash, is preserved for debugging but excluded from the environment compatibility fingerprint.

The default conflict policy is to fail when an existing environment name has a different fingerprint. Use explicit policies for intentional changes:

wet.env("analysis", spec, on_conflict="reuse")
wet.env("analysis", spec, on_conflict="update")
wet.env("analysis", spec, on_conflict="recreate")

Only owned environments can be updated or recreated. Attached paths are protected from destructive mutation by default.

docs.title: Private Pip Indexes
docs.text:
Use pip_index_url when pip dependencies must be installed from a private or alternate package index:

spec = EnvSpec(
    pip=["private-package"],
    pip_index_url="https://packages.example.invalid/simple",
)

pip_index_url must not contain credentials. Put authentication in the package tooling or environment outside the Wetlands spec; Wetlands rejects URLs such as https://user:token@example.invalid/simple before serializing the spec to registry, service, or command metadata.

For the micromamba backend, Wetlands passes the URL to pip install with --index-url for normal pip installs and no-dependency pip installs. Pixi support is narrower: the Pixi no-deps fallback can use pip install --index-url, but normal Pixi pypi installs do not support pip_index_url; that path is not supported and fails explicitly instead of generating an invalid Pixi command.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Start with WetlandsSession when the current Python process should own the workers.

from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsSession

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy==2.2.4"]), workers=1)
    print(env.call("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([1, 2, 3],)))

The root directory stores Wetlands metadata, environment records, service metadata, and debug metadata. The context manager stops session-owned workers when it exits; it does not delete the environment directory.

docs.title: Create An Environment
docs.text:
from wetlands import EnvSpec, PackageSpec, WetlandsSession

spec = EnvSpec(
    python="3.12",
    conda=[PackageSpec("numpy", "==2.2.4")],
    channels=["conda-forge"],
)

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("analysis", spec, workers=2)

You can also load a spec file in the caller process:

spec = EnvSpec.from_file("requirements.txt")
spec = EnvSpec.from_file("environment.yml")
spec = EnvSpec.from_file("pyproject.toml", optional_dependencies=["analysis"])
spec = EnvSpec.from_file("pixi.toml", environment="default")

Wetlands records the normalized spec and fingerprint. If an environment name already exists with a different fingerprint, wet.env() raises by default. The explicit conflict policies are:

  • on_conflict="error": fail on mismatch.
  • on_conflict="reuse": keep the existing environment.
  • on_conflict="update": update an owned environment.
  • on_conflict="recreate": replace an owned environment.

Attached or accepted-untracked paths are protected from destructive update, recreate, and delete operations unless you opt in with the documented force flags.

docs.title: Call Code
docs.text:
result = env.call("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([1, 2, 3],))

task = env.submit("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([4, 5, 6],))
print(task.wait().result)

module = env.module("examples/minimal_module.py")
print(module.sum([7, 8, 9]))

Use env.call() for direct blocking work. Use env.submit() when you need task status, cancellation, or progress fields. In session-owned environments, local Task objects also support listeners and a future-like object. Use env.module() in session mode when a module proxy is clearer than naming the function each time. Service-owned environments currently expose call, submit, map, submit_many, run_script, run, and spawn; they do not implement the module proxy yet.

docs.title: Batch Work
docs.text:
items = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]

results = list(env.map("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", items))
tasks = env.submit_many("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", items)
finished = [task.wait().result for task in tasks]

Set workers=N when the environment is created, or call env.scale(workers=N) later.

docs.title: Run Scripts And Commands
docs.text:
env.run_script("scripts/report.py", args=("input.tif",))

completed = env.run(["python", "-V"])
print(completed.returncode, completed.stdout)

process = env.spawn(["python", "-u", "server.py"])
process.wait(timeout=10)
print(process.logs())

env.run() returns a serializable completed-process result. env.spawn() returns a process proxy for longer-running commands.

docs.title: Persistent Service
docs.text:
Use a service when environment and task metadata should outlive one client object:

from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsService, connect

WetlandsService.ensure_running(root="wetlands")

wet = connect(root="wetlands")
env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy==2.2.4"]), workers=2)
print(env.call("examples/minimal_module.py", "sum", args=([1, 2, 3],)))
wet.close()

Current service semantics are intentionally narrow:

  • the transport is local and in-process;
  • connect() requires a live endpoint in the current Python process;
  • completed task records are retained in memory according to service retention settings;
  • task and process records are not durable across service stop or process exit.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Wetlands separates environment state, worker ownership, task dispatch, process execution, and service metadata.

docs.title: Session Ownership
docs.text:
WetlandsSession owns workers in the current Python process.

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy"]), workers=2)
    env.call("work.py", "run")

When the session closes, session-owned workers and spawned processes are stopped. The environment directory remains on disk unless you explicitly delete it.

docs.title: Service Ownership
docs.text:
WetlandsService owns service state. Clients connect with connect() and receive proxies for environments, tasks, and processes.

WetlandsService.ensure_running(root="wetlands")
wet = connect(root="wetlands")

Current service metadata is written under the Wetlands root and includes a config digest, service id, endpoint metadata, and auth key path. The current endpoint is an in-process transport, not a real background daemon or cross-process IPC service. If metadata points to a live process but no in-process endpoint exists, connect() fails instead of using stale metadata.

docs.title: Workers And Tasks
docs.text:
Environments can keep one or more warm workers. Function calls are dispatched to workers. If all workers are busy, tasks queue until a worker is available.

env.scale(workers=4)
tasks = env.submit_many("segment.py", "segment", images)

Task state includes status, result, error, traceback, progress fields, and outputs. In service mode this state is retained in memory according to retention settings. It is not durable across service stop.

docs.title: Processes
docs.text:
env.run() executes a command and returns a completed-process result. env.spawn() starts a longer-running command and returns a process proxy.

Process records are currently retained in memory by the owning session or service.

docs.title: Current Implementation Boundaries
docs.text:
• service transport: in-process only;
  • task and process stores: in-memory only;
  • task logs: not implemented;
  • real daemon log stream: not implemented;
  • local IPC/auth design: trusted local machine, not internet-facing.

docs.title: Lifecycle And Cleanup
docs.text: Wetlands 2 makes ownership explicit.

docs.title: Session-Owned
docs.text:
WetlandsSession is for bounded work:

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("numpy", spec, workers=1)
    env.call("work.py", "run")

When the context exits, Wetlands stops session-owned workers and spawned processes. It does not delete environment directories by default.

docs.title: Service-Owned
docs.text:
WetlandsService owns environments and task records independently of one client handle:

WetlandsService.ensure_running(root="wetlands")
wet = connect(root="wetlands")
wet.close()
WetlandsService(root="wetlands").stop()

Closing a client disconnects that client only. Stopping the service releases service workers, spawned processes, and in-memory task/process records.

The current service transport is in-process only. It is useful for exercising the public service/client API shape, but it is not yet a durable background daemon.

Stopping an environment is non-force by default:

env.stop()

Non-force stop refuses while the environment has pending or running tasks. Use env.stop(force=True) only when you intentionally want unfinished tasks failed as part of shutdown. The TUI monitor exposes only the non-force stop path and requires exact environment-name confirmation before sending it.

docs.title: Environment Mutation Safety
docs.text:
Environment creation records ownership:

  • owned: Wetlands created the environment path;
  • attached: the path was provided by the user;
  • accepted-untracked: Wetlands accepted an existing path after explicit conflict policy.

Owned environments can be updated, recreated, or deleted with explicit calls:

env.update(new_spec)
env.recreate(new_spec)
env.delete(force=True)

Attached paths are protected. Deleting attached paths requires explicit force and attached-delete intent:

env.delete(force=True, delete_attached=True)

docs.title: CLI Cleanup
docs.text:
wetlands service stop --root wetlands
wetlands cleanup --root wetlands
wetlands cleanup --all

cleanup refuses to clean a root while a service is reported as running. Use it for orphaned worker processes, not as a replacement for normal service shutdown.

docs.title: Logging
docs.text: Wetlands 2 separates process results, task state, service status, and future durable log streams.

docs.title: Process Output
docs.text:
Use env.run() for commands where stdout and stderr should be captured with the result:

completed = env.run(["python", "-c", "print('ready')"])
print(completed.stdout)
print(completed.stderr)
print(completed.logs)

Use env.spawn() for longer-running commands:

process = env.spawn(["python", "-u", "server.py"])
process.wait(timeout=30)
print(process.logs())

Session mode stores spawned process output on the process proxy after it exits. Service mode exposes process logs through the remote process proxy while the service keeps that in-memory process record.

docs.title: Task State
docs.text:
Tasks expose structured state:

task = env.submit("analysis.py", "run", args=("input.tif",))
task.wait()
print(task.status, task.error, task.traceback)

The current service scaffold does not provide task log streams. CLI task logs reports that logs are unavailable. Service task snapshots include log_capability with available: false and reason task-logs-unavailable.

docs.title: Service Logs
docs.text:
wetlands service status --root wetlands reports the service metadata Wetlands currently knows about. wetlands service logs --root wetlands only prints a configured log file when one exists. The current in-process service transport has no real daemon log stream, so service logs may be unavailable.

Applications that need durable logs today should write application logs to explicit files or return structured output from tasks.

docs.title: Manual Communication And Processes
docs.text: Most code should use env.call(), env.submit(), and env.map() for Python functions. Session-owned environments may also use env.module() for a dynamic blocking module proxy. Use env.run() or env.spawn() when the environment should run a whole process.

docs.title: Blocking Commands
docs.text:
completed = env.run(["python", "-V"], timeout=30)
print(completed.returncode)
print(completed.stdout)

env.run() strips parent Python environment variables by default so the child process uses the selected environment instead of accidental parent interpreter settings.

docs.title: Long-Running Processes
docs.text:
process = env.spawn(["python", "-u", "examples/manual_communication_module.py"])

try:
    process.wait(timeout=10)
finally:
    if process.returncode is None:
        process.terminate()

print(process.status())
print(process.logs())

In session mode, env.spawn() wraps a local subprocess. In service mode, it returns a remote process proxy. Process records are currently in-memory only; they are not durable across service restarts.

docs.title: Custom Protocols
docs.text: If you need a custom protocol, start the protocol server with env.spawn() and connect to it yourself using the protocol of your choice. Keep cleanup explicit: terminate or kill the process when the protocol server is no longer needed, and make sure any application-level resources are released by the server before it exits.

docs.title: Migration Notes
docs.text:
This page is historical migration material. The names below are not the primary Wetlands 2 API.

Wetlands 1 concept Wetlands 2 concept EnvironmentManager(...) WetlandsSession(...) or WetlandsService plus connect() wetlands_instance_path root create(name, dependencies) wet.env(name, EnvSpec(...)) create_from_config(...) wet.env(name, EnvSpec.from_file(...)) load(name, path) wet.attach_env(name, path, spec=...) env.launch(max_workers=N) wet.env(..., workers=N) or env.scale(workers=N) env.execute(module, function, ...) env.call(module, function, args=..., kwargs=...) env.import_module(path) env.module(path) map_tasks(...) env.submit_many(...) execute_commands(...) env.run(...) or env.spawn(...) env.exit() env.stop() or session/service close wetlands list wetlands debug list wetlands kill wetlands env stop or wetlands cleanup

Backward compatibility with the old public names is not a Wetlands 2 design goal. Some old implementation modules still exist internally while the rewrite is staged, but new docs and examples should use the Wetlands 2 names.

docs.title: Typical Rewrite
docs.text:
Historical:

from wetlands.environment_manager import EnvironmentManager

manager = EnvironmentManager()
env = manager.create("numpy", {"pip": ["numpy"]})
env.launch()
result = env.execute("maths.py", "total", args=([1, 2, 3],))
env.exit()

Wetlands 2:

from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsSession

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("numpy", EnvSpec(pip=["numpy"]), workers=1)
    result = env.call("maths.py", "total", args=([1, 2, 3],))

docs.title: Paths
docs.text:
Wetlands 2 keeps path handling explicit so code can move from session mode to service mode without hidden rewrites.

Raw string arguments passed to your functions are not rewritten. If a value is a data path, pass the path your remote function should see or use a structured path reference.

from wetlands import EnvSpec, WetlandsSession, path

spec = EnvSpec.from_file(path.spec("requirements.txt"))

with WetlandsSession(root="wetlands") as wet:
    env = wet.env("analysis", spec)
    env.call("analysis.py", "run", args=(path.project("data/input.tif"),))

docs.title: Path Roles
docs.text:
The path namespace creates PathRef values with intent:

path.module("analysis.py")
path.script("scripts/report.py")
path.spec("requirements.txt")
path.cwd(".")
path.project("data/input.tif")

EnvSpec.from_file(path.spec("requirements.txt")) accepts spec path refs. Function and command calls can pass path refs in arguments when application code understands those structured values.

docs.title: Service Semantics
docs.text:
In service mode, the client parses spec files before sending the normalized EnvSpec to the service. This avoids asking the service to read paths that may exist only on a client machine.

For remote-ready code:

  • keep module, script, spec, cwd, and data paths distinct;
  • avoid relying on implicit current working directories;
  • treat arbitrary strings as application data, not paths Wetlands should rewrite.

Project is exported as a path-model building block, but project mapping is not wired into WetlandsSession, WetlandsService, or connect() yet. Treat client/service root translation as future work unless application code handles the mapping explicitly.

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text: Wetlands 2 is designed for trusted local execution.

docs.title: Service Transport
docs.text:
The current service endpoint is an in-process local transport. It is not a network daemon, not a remote multi-user service, and not suitable for exposure to untrusted clients.

Service metadata under the Wetlands root records the service id, config digest, endpoint metadata, and auth key path. connect() checks that the requested config is compatible with the running service metadata before creating a client.

docs.title: Auth Key Scope
docs.text:
Wetlands creates an auth key under the service root with restrictive local file permissions. The auth key is scoped to that Wetlands root and service metadata. It is a local guard against accidental or incompatible client attachment, not a complete security boundary for untrusted users on a shared machine.

Treat the root directory as sensitive. Anyone who can read or modify the root metadata can interfere with the service lifecycle.

docs.title: Monitor Output
docs.text:
wetlands monitor is a trusted local diagnostic command. Its JSON and details output are sanitized by default: raw service metadata, raw discovery snapshots, auth key paths, task args, task results, task tracebacks, process env payloads, and process output payloads are omitted or redacted where the monitor model can identify them.

wetlands monitor --tui uses the same sanitized model for navigation. It may launch an IDE attach for the selected eligible debug target, and it may request a non-force service stop for the selected environment after exact-name confirmation. It does not add TUI delete, cleanup, kill, or prune actions.

This redaction is hygiene for trusted local workflows, not a security boundary. Monitor output still reports local paths, environment names, PIDs, cwd values, status fields, capability reasons, log capability, and redacted command summaries. Do not expose the current monitor output to untrusted users or remote clients.

docs.title: Pickle And Code Execution
docs.text: Wetlands sends Python objects and executes Python functions in selected environments. Only run code and accept task inputs from trusted sources. Do not expose the current transport over a network or to untrusted clients without a different transport, serialization, authentication, authorization, and audit design.

docs.title: Worker IPC
docs.text: Workers are local processes owned by the same user workflow. The current worker IPC model is trusted-local. Use operating-system permissions and project isolation to protect the Wetlands root, source files, environment directories, and shared-memory handles.

docs.title: Shared Memory
docs.text:
Wetlands supports shared-memory ndarray transfer through wetlands.ndarray.NDArray. Use it when copying large NumPy arrays through task serialization would be too expensive.

from wetlands.ndarray import NDArray

with NDArray(array=image) as shared:
    result = env.call("array_ops.py", "measure", args=(shared,))

Remote code receives an NDArray handle and accesses handle.array.

def measure(image):
    return float(image.array.mean())

docs.title: Ownership
docs.text:
The process that creates an NDArray owns the shared-memory block unless ownership is explicitly transferred by application logic. Close views when a process is done with them. Unlink blocks from the owning side when the data is no longer needed.

from wetlands.ndarray import NDArray

with NDArray(shape=(1024, 1024), dtype="float32") as output:
    env.call("array_ops.py", "fill", args=(output,))
    local_copy = output.array.copy()

docs.title: Service Mode
docs.text:
Service-owned tasks may retain results while task retention keeps them reachable. Shared memory referenced by retained task results must stay alive until the task is forgotten or pruned. The current implementation keeps this state in memory only. It does not provide a durable shared-memory ownership database across service restarts.

For very large data or long retention windows, prefer explicit files or application-level shared-memory handles with a cleanup policy your application owns.

docs.title: Tasks And Parallel Execution
docs.text:
env.submit() returns a task object. env.map() and env.submit_many() build on the same worker pool semantics for repeated calls.

task = env.submit("compute.py", "heavy", args=(payload,))
task.wait(timeout=60)
result = task.result

All task handles expose status, result, error, progress fields, cancellation, and wait(timeout=None). Session-owned local Task objects also support listeners, a future, and awaitable behavior. Service-owned RemoteTask objects currently use a polling subset and do not expose listeners, future, or await.

def on_event(event):
    print(event.type.value, event.task.status.value)

task = env.submit("compute.py", "heavy", args=(payload,))
task.listen(on_event)
result = task.wait().result

The listener example above is session-local today. In service mode, inspect retained state with task.snapshot(), client.task(task_id), and client.tasks.list().

Remote functions can accept a task handle when they want to publish progress or check for cooperative cancellation.

def heavy(items, task=None):
    total = 0
    for index, item in enumerate(items, start=1):
        if task is not None and task.cancel_requested:
            return total
        total += item
        if task is not None:
            task.update(message="working", current=index, maximum=len(items))
    return total

docs.title: Deferred Start
docs.text:
task = env.submit("compute.py", "heavy", args=(payload,), start=False)
task.start()
print(task.wait().result)

Use deferred start when a caller needs to configure a task before dispatch. In session-owned environments, that can include attaching local task listeners before the task starts.

docs.title: Batch Results
docs.text:
env.map() yields results:

images = ["a.tif", "b.tif", "c.tif"]
results = list(env.map("segment.py", "segment", images, ordered=True))

env.submit_many() returns task handles:

tasks = env.submit_many("segment.py", "segment", images)
for task in tasks:
    print(task.wait().result)

docs.title: Session And Service Retention
docs.text:
Session-owned tasks are owned by the current Python process. When the session closes, workers stop and in-process task handles are no longer useful.

Service-owned tasks are retained by the service in memory. A client may disconnect and a later client may inspect retained tasks while the same service endpoint remains alive. Retention is bounded by service settings such as task retention time and maximum retained results. There is currently no durable task store, so retained task state disappears when the service stops.

docs.title: Logs
docs.text: Task status, result, error, traceback, progress, and outputs are part of the task model. A task log stream is not implemented in the current service scaffold. CLI task logs fails clearly instead of pretending durable logs exist.

docs.title: WetlandsClient
docs.text:
Connected handle to a persistent Wetlands service.

Source code in wetlands/client.py 
class WetlandsClient:
    """Connected handle to a persistent Wetlands service."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        config: WetlandsConfig | None = None,
        *,
        service_metadata: dict[str, object] | None = None,
        **options: object,
    ) -> None:
        self.config = WetlandsConfig.from_options(config, **options)
        self.env = RemoteEnvironmentCollection(self)  # type: ignore[method-assign]
        self.process = RemoteProcessCollection(self)  # type: ignore[method-assign]
        self.debug = RemoteDebugCollection(self)
        self.tasks = RemoteTaskCollection(self)
        self._connected = service_metadata is not None
        self._transport: rpc.LocalTransport | None = None
        if service_metadata is not None:
            endpoint_metadata = service_metadata.get("endpoint")
            service_id = service_metadata.get("service_id")
            if (
                isinstance(endpoint_metadata, dict)
                and endpoint_metadata.get("transport") == "in-process"
                and isinstance(service_id, str)
            ):
                endpoint = rpc.local_endpoint(service_id)
                if endpoint is not None:
                    self._transport = rpc.LocalTransport(
                        endpoint,
                        auth_key=_read_service_auth_key(self.config.root, service_metadata),
                    )
        self._connected_config = MappingProxyType(
            {
                "service_id": service_metadata.get("service_id") if service_metadata else None,
                "pid": service_metadata.get("pid") if service_metadata else None,
                "endpoint": service_metadata.get("endpoint") if service_metadata else None,
                "config": service_metadata.get("config") if service_metadata else self.config.to_dict(),
                "config_digest": service_metadata.get("config_digest")
                if service_metadata
                else config_compatibility_digest(self.config),
                "metadata": service_metadata,
            }
        )

    @property
    def connected(self) -> bool:
        return self._connected

    @property
    def connected_config(self) -> Mapping[str, object]:
        return self._connected_config

    def close(self) -> None:
        if self._connected and self._transport is not None:
            try:
                self._transport.request("client.disconnect", {})
            except rpc.RpcError:
                pass
        self._connected = False

    def env(
        self,
        name: str,
        spec: EnvSpec,
        workers: int = 1,
        on_conflict: str = "error",
        auto_start: bool = True,
        **options: Any,
    ) -> Environment:
        if options:
            unexpected = next(iter(options))
            raise TypeError(f"Unexpected env option: {unexpected}")
        if not isinstance(spec, EnvSpec):
            raise TypeError("spec must be an EnvSpec")
        result = self._request(
            "env.ensure",
            {
                "name": name,
                "spec": spec.to_dict(),
                "workers": workers,
                "on_conflict": on_conflict,
                "auto_start": auto_start,
            },
        )
        if not isinstance(result, dict):
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Service returned malformed env.ensure response")
        return _ServiceEnvironmentProxy(self, result)

    def attach_env(
        self,
        name: str,
        path: str | Path,
        spec: EnvSpec | None = None,
        workers: int = 0,
        auto_start: bool = True,
        **options: Any,
    ) -> Environment:
        if options:
            unexpected = next(iter(options))
            raise TypeError(f"Unexpected attach_env option: {unexpected}")
        if spec is not None and not isinstance(spec, EnvSpec):
            raise TypeError("spec must be an EnvSpec")
        result = self._request(
            "env.attach",
            {
                "name": name,
                "path": str(path),
                "spec": spec.to_dict() if spec is not None else None,
                "workers": workers,
                "auto_start": auto_start,
            },
        )
        if not isinstance(result, dict):
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Service returned malformed env.attach response")
        return _ServiceEnvironmentProxy(self, result)

    def task(self, task_id: str) -> RemoteTask:
        result = self._request("task.status", {"task_id": task_id})
        if not isinstance(result, dict):
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Service returned malformed task.status response")
        return RemoteTask(self, result)

    def process(self, process_id: str) -> RemoteProcess:
        result = self._request("process.status", {"process_id": process_id})
        if not isinstance(result, dict):
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Service returned malformed process.status response")
        return ServiceRemoteProcess(self, result)

    def _request(self, operation: str, payload: dict[str, object]) -> object:
        if not self._connected:
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Wetlands client is closed")
        if self._transport is None:
            raise ServiceConnectionError("Wetlands service endpoint is not available in this process")
        try:
            return self._transport.request(operation, payload)
        except rpc.RpcRemoteError as exc:
            if exc.code == "process_timeout":
                raise TimeoutError(str(exc)) from exc
            raise ServiceConnectionError(f"{exc.code}: {exc}") from exc
        except rpc.RpcError as exc:
            raise ServiceConnectionError(str(exc)) from exc

class NDArray:
    """
    NDArray: A wrapper around a numpy array stored in shared memory.
    Pickles into a small dict containing shared memory metadata.

    Can be initialized in two ways:
    1. With an array: NDArray(array=my_array) - creates shared memory and copies data
    2. With shape and dtype: NDArray(shape=(100, 100), dtype='float32') -
       creates shared memory but defers numpy array creation until first access (lazy)
       WARNING: the numpy array values will be UNDEFINED,
                you MUST set `array.fill(0)` or `array[:] = otherArray` before using it

    Args:
        array: numpy array to wrap (mutually exclusive with shape/dtype)
        shm: existing SharedMemory object to use (optional)
        shape: shape of array for lazy initialization (requires dtype)
        dtype: dtype of array for lazy initialization (requires shape)
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        array: np.ndarray | None = None,
        shm: shared_memory.SharedMemory | None = None,
        shape: tuple | None = None,
        dtype: str | type | None = None,
    ):
        if array is not None:
            if shm is None:
                # Allocate shared memory
                shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(create=True, size=array.nbytes)
                # Copy array data into shared memory
                shm_arr = np.ndarray(array.shape, dtype=array.dtype, buffer=shm.buf)
                shm_arr[:] = array[:]
            else:
                # Use existing shared memory
                shm_arr = np.ndarray(array.shape, dtype=array.dtype, buffer=shm.buf)
            self._array = shm_arr
            self.shape = array.shape
            self.dtype = array.dtype
        else:
            # Lazy initialization with shape and dtype
            if shape is None or dtype is None:
                raise ValueError("Either 'array' or both 'shape' and 'dtype' must be provided")
            resolveddtype = np.dtype(dtype) if not isinstance(dtype, np.dtype) else dtype
            if shm is None:
                # Allocate shared memory
                size = int(np.prod(shape) * resolveddtype.itemsize)
                shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(create=True, size=size)
            self._array = None
            self.shape = shape
            self.dtype = resolveddtype

        self.shm = shm

    @property
    def array(self) -> np.ndarray:
        """Lazily create the numpy array from shared memory on first access."""
        if self._array is None:
            self._array = np.ndarray(self.shape, dtype=self.dtype, buffer=self.shm.buf)
        return self._array

    def __reduce__(self):
        """
        What the object becomes when pickled.
        Returns a tuple describing how to reconstruct the object:
         (callable, args)
        """
        assert self.shm is not None, "shm must not be None for pickling"
        state = {"name": self.shm.name, "shape": self.shape, "dtype": str(self.dtype)}

        return (self._reconstruct, (state,))

    @staticmethod
    def _reconstruct(state):
        """
        Rebuilds the NDArray when unpickled.
        """

        # Child process track shared memory and unlink them on exit
        # In Wetlands, the owner is the process which created the NDArray
        # So we want to unregister the shm when unpickling
        try:
            # For Python >= 3.13, disable tracking (unlink when process exists) with track=False
            shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=state["name"], track=False)
        except TypeError:
            # For Python < 3.13, unregister manually
            shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=state["name"])
            resource_tracker.unregister(shm._name, "shared_memory")  # type: ignore
        array = np.ndarray(state["shape"], dtype=np.dtype(state["dtype"]), buffer=shm.buf)
        return NDArray(array, shm=shm)

    def close(self):
        """Close shared memory view (but keep block alive)."""
        if self.shm is not None:
            self.shm.close()

    def unlink(self):
        """Free the shared memory block."""
        if self.shm is not None:
            self.shm.unlink()

    def unregister(self):
        # Unregister the shared memory for the resource tracker
        if self.shm is not None:
            with suppress(Exception):
                resource_tracker.unregister(self.shm._name, "shared_memory")  # type: ignore

    def dispose(self, unregister=False):
        """Close, free, and optionally unregister the shared memory block."""
        # with suppress(Exception):
        self.close()

        # with suppress(Exception):
        self.unlink()

        if unregister:
            with suppress(Exception):
                self.unregister()

    def __enter__(self):
        """Enter context manager."""
        return self

    def __exit__(self, _exc_type, _exc_val, _exc_tb):
        """Exit context manager and cleanup resources."""
        self.dispose()
        return False

    def __repr__(self):
        shm_name = self.shm.name if self.shm is not None else "None"
        return f"NDArray(shape={self.shape}, dtype={self.dtype}, shm={shm_name})"

def register_ndarray_pickle():
    """
    Register NDArray pickling with the Python copyreg framework.
    Users call this manually when they want NDArray to be picklable.
    """

    global _registered
    if _registered:
        return

    copyreg.pickle(NDArray, _pickle_ndarray)
    _registered = True

def update_ndarray(
    array: np.ndarray | None = None,
    ndarray: NDArray | None = None,
    shape: tuple | None = None,
    dtype: str | type | None = None,
):
    """updates ndarray from array:
    if ndarray is None: create an NDArray from array
    else:
    if dtype and shape are same of ndarray: return ndarray
    else:
        if array has the same shape and size as ndarray:
            update the ndarray values and return it
        else: delete the ndarray and create a new one from array
    """
    if ndarray is not None:
        if shape == ndarray.shape and dtype == ndarray.dtype:
            return ndarray
        if array is not None and ndarray.dtype == array.dtype and ndarray.shape == array.shape:
            ndarray.array[:] = array[:]
            return ndarray
        ndarray.dispose()
        ndarray = None
    if array is not None:
        return NDArray(array)
    else:
        return NDArray(shape=shape, dtype=dtype)

def strip_python_environment_vars(
    env: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    *,
    remove: bool = True,
) -> dict[str, str]:
    """Return a copy of env without parent Python overrides by default."""
    clean_env = dict(os.environ if env is None else env)
    if not remove:
        return clean_env
    for name in PYTHON_ENVIRONMENT_VARS:
        clean_env.pop(name, None)
    return clean_env

def run(
    command: list[str | os.PathLike[str]] | str,
    *,
    timeout: float | None = None,
    cwd: str | Path | None = None,
    env: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    remove_python_env_vars: bool = True,
) -> CompletedProcess:
    """Run a local command and return the Wetlands 2 process result shape."""
    args = _validate_command(command)
    process_env = strip_python_environment_vars(env, remove=remove_python_env_vars)
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            args,
            capture_output=True,
            cwd=cwd,
            env=process_env,
            shell=isinstance(args, str),
            text=True,
            timeout=timeout,
            check=False,
        )
    except subprocess.TimeoutExpired as exc:
        stdout = _normalize_output(exc.stdout)
        stderr = _normalize_output(exc.stderr)
        return CompletedProcess(
            args=args,
            returncode=-1,
            stdout=stdout,
            stderr=stderr,
            logs=_logs_from_output(stdout, stderr),
            timed_out=True,
        )
    return CompletedProcess(
        args=result.args,
        returncode=result.returncode,
        stdout=result.stdout,
        stderr=result.stderr,
        logs=_logs_from_output(result.stdout, result.stderr),
        timed_out=False,
    )

class WetlandsService:
    """Controller for persistent Wetlands service state and lifecycle."""

    def __init__(self, config: WetlandsConfig | None = None, **options: object) -> None:
        self._backend = options.pop("_backend", None)
        self.config = WetlandsConfig.from_options(config, **options)
        self._service_id: str | None = None

    @classmethod
    def ensure_running(cls, *args: object, **kwargs: object) -> WetlandsService:
        service = cls(*args, **kwargs)
        metadata = read_service_metadata(service.config.root)
        if metadata is not None and pid_is_alive(metadata.get("pid")):
            if metadata.get("config_digest") != config_compatibility_digest(service.config):
                raise RuntimeError(f"Requested Wetlands service config is incompatible with {service.config.root}")
            if _requires_local_endpoint(metadata) and _local_endpoint_for_metadata(metadata) is None:
                raise RuntimeError(
                    f"Wetlands service metadata for {service.config.root} points to a live process "
                    "but no local in-process endpoint is registered"
                )
            service._service_id = str(metadata.get("service_id"))
            return service
        service.start()
        return service

    def start(self, background: bool = True) -> None:
        _ = background
        metadata = read_service_metadata(self.config.root)
        if metadata is not None and pid_is_alive(metadata.get("pid")):
            if metadata.get("config_digest") != config_compatibility_digest(self.config):
                raise RuntimeError(f"Requested Wetlands service config is incompatible with {self.config.root}")
            service_id = str(metadata.get("service_id"))
            if _local_endpoint_for_metadata(metadata) is not None:
                self._service_id = service_id
                return
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Wetlands service metadata for {self.config.root} points to a live process "
                "but no local in-process endpoint is registered"
            )
        if metadata is not None:
            with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
                service_metadata_path(self.config.root).unlink()
        service_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
        owner = RuntimeOwner.acquire(self.config.root, kind="service", runtime_id=service_id)
        endpoint = _LocalServiceEndpoint(
            config=self.config,
            service_id=service_id,
            owner=owner,
            backend=self._backend,
        )
        rpc.register_local_endpoint(service_id, endpoint)
        ensure_auth_key(self.config.root)
        write_service_metadata(
            self.config.root,
            service_id=service_id,
            pid=os.getpid(),
            config=self.config,
            endpoint={"transport": "in-process", "service_id": service_id},
        )
        self._service_id = service_id

    def status(self) -> dict[str, object]:
        metadata = read_service_metadata(self.config.root)
        if metadata is None:
            return {
                "running": False,
                "state": "not-running",
                "root": str(self.config.root),
                "service": None,
                "stale": None,
            }
        if pid_is_alive(metadata.get("pid")):
            if _requires_local_endpoint(metadata) and _local_endpoint_for_metadata(metadata) is None:
                return {
                    "running": False,
                    "state": "unavailable",
                    "root": str(self.config.root),
                    "service": None,
                    "stale": {
                        "reason": "endpoint-unavailable",
                        "service_id": metadata.get("service_id"),
                        "pid": metadata.get("pid"),
                        "metadata": metadata,
                    },
                }
            return {
                "running": True,
                "state": "running",
                "root": str(self.config.root),
                "service": metadata,
                "stale": None,
            }
        return {
            "running": False,
            "state": "stale",
            "root": str(self.config.root),
            "service": None,
            "stale": {
                "reason": "process-not-running",
                "service_id": metadata.get("service_id"),
                "pid": metadata.get("pid"),
                "metadata": metadata,
            },
        }

    def stop(self) -> None:
        metadata = read_service_metadata(self.config.root)
        service_id = self._service_id or (str(metadata.get("service_id")) if metadata else None)
        endpoint = _local_endpoint_for_metadata(metadata) if metadata is not None else None
        if (
            endpoint is None
            and metadata is not None
            and pid_is_alive(metadata.get("pid"))
            and _requires_local_endpoint(metadata)
        ):
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Wetlands service endpoint is not available in this process for {self.config.root}; "
                "refusing to delete live service metadata"
            )
        if endpoint is None and service_id is not None:
            endpoint = rpc.local_endpoint(service_id)
        if isinstance(endpoint, _LocalServiceEndpoint):
            endpoint.shutdown()
            rpc.unregister_local_endpoint(service_id)
        if metadata is not None:
            with suppress(FileNotFoundError):
                service_metadata_path(self.config.root).unlink()
        self._service_id = None

class WetlandsSession:
    """Session-owned runtime.

    This bounded implementation owns the current process root marker and adapts
    configuration into the existing manager. Environment creation and worker dispatch are
    implemented by later runtime phases.
    """

    def __init__(self, config: WetlandsConfig | None = None, **options: object) -> None:
        backend = options.pop("_backend", None)
        self.config = WetlandsConfig.from_options(config, **options)
        self._owner: RuntimeOwner | None = RuntimeOwner.acquire(self.config.root, kind="session")
        try:
            self._manager = EnvironmentManager(**self.config.to_environment_manager_kwargs())
        except Exception:
            self._release_owner()
            raise
        self._registry = EnvironmentRegistry(self.config.root)
        self._backend: _EnvironmentBackend = (
            backend
            if backend is not None
            else _ManagerBackend(self._manager, main_env_policy=self.config.main_env_policy)
        )
        self._session_environments: dict[str, _SessionEnvironment] = {}
        self.closed = False

    def __enter__(self) -> WetlandsSession:
        return self

    def __exit__(self, exc_type: object, exc: object, traceback: object) -> None:
        self.close()

    def close(self) -> None:
        if self.closed:
            return
        try:
            self._stop_owned_environments()
        finally:
            self._release_owner()
            self.closed = True

    def env(
        self,
        name: str,
        spec: EnvSpec,
        workers: int = 1,
        on_conflict: ConflictPolicy = "error",
        auto_start: bool = True,
        **options: Any,
    ) -> object:
        validate_environment_name(name)
        self._validate_spec(spec)
        self._validate_workers(workers)
        self._validate_conflict_policy(on_conflict)
        self._validate_auto_start(auto_start)
        self._ensure_root_owned_before_starting(workers)
        _ = options
        entry = self._registry_entry(name)
        path = self._backend.environment_path(name)
        requested_fingerprint = spec.fingerprint()

        if entry is None:
            if self._backend.exists(name):
                path = self._acquire_untracked(name, spec, workers, on_conflict, auto_start)
                ownership: Ownership = "owned" if on_conflict == "recreate" else "accepted-untracked"
            else:
                path, ownership = self._create_owned(name, spec, workers, auto_start)
            return self._environment_handle(name, path, spec, ownership, workers, auto_start)

        path = Path(entry["path"])
        entry_fingerprint = entry.get("spec_fingerprint")
        ownership = entry.get("owner", "owned")
        if ownership not in {"owned", "attached", "accepted-untracked"}:
            ownership = "owned"
        ownership = ownership  # type: ignore[assignment]
        existing_spec = EnvSpec.from_dict(entry["spec"]) if isinstance(entry.get("spec"), dict) else None

        if entry_fingerprint == requested_fingerprint:
            self._validate_existing_entry_for_policy(name, path)
            self._registry.record_acquisition(name, desired_workers=workers, auto_start=auto_start)
            return self._environment_handle(name, path, spec, ownership, workers, auto_start)

        if on_conflict == "error":
            raise EnvironmentConflictError(
                f"Environment '{name}' conflicts with requested spec fingerprint {requested_fingerprint}"
            )
        if on_conflict == "reuse":
            self._validate_existing_entry_for_policy(name, path)
            self._registry.record_acquisition(name, desired_workers=workers, auto_start=auto_start)
            return self._environment_handle(name, path, existing_spec or spec, ownership, workers, auto_start)
        if ownership != "owned":
            raise EnvironmentConflictError(
                f"Environment '{name}' is {ownership}; refusing destructive conflict policy '{on_conflict}'"
            )
        self._validate_existing_entry_for_policy(name, path)
        if on_conflict == "update":
            path = self._update_owned(name, spec, path, workers, auto_start)
            return self._environment_handle(name, path, spec, "owned", workers, auto_start)

        path = self._recreate_owned(name, spec, path, workers, auto_start)
        return self._environment_handle(name, path, spec, "owned", workers, auto_start)

    def attach_env(
        self,
        name: str,
        path: str | Path,
        spec: EnvSpec | None = None,
        workers: int = 0,
        auto_start: bool = True,
        **options: Any,
    ) -> object:
        validate_environment_name(name)
        if spec is not None:
            self._validate_spec(spec)
        self._validate_workers(workers)
        self._validate_auto_start(auto_start)
        self._ensure_root_owned_before_starting(workers)
        _ = options
        environment_path = Path(path)
        if not environment_path.exists():
            raise FileNotFoundError(f"Attached environment path does not exist: {environment_path}")
        self._registry.record_success(
            name,
            path=environment_path,
            spec=spec,
            ownership="attached",
            backend=self._backend.name,
            desired_workers=workers,
            auto_start=auto_start,
        )
        return self._environment_handle(name, environment_path, spec, "attached", workers, auto_start)

    @property
    def manager(self) -> EnvironmentManager:
        return self._manager

    def _release_owner(self) -> None:
        if self._owner is None:
            return
        self._owner.release()
        self._owner = None

    def _ensure_root_owned_before_starting(self, workers: int) -> None:
        if workers == 0:
            return
        if self._owner is None:
            raise RootOwnershipError("Root ownership is required before starting workers")
        marker = self._owner.root / "runtime_owner.json"
        with root_lock(self._owner.root):
            try:
                payload = json.loads(marker.read_text())
            except FileNotFoundError as exc:
                raise RootOwnershipError("Root ownership marker is missing; refusing to start workers") from exc
            except json.JSONDecodeError as exc:
                raise RootOwnershipError("Root ownership marker is invalid; refusing to start workers") from exc
            if payload.get("runtime_id") != self._owner.runtime_id:
                raise RootOwnershipError("Root ownership marker belongs to another runtime; refusing to start workers")

    def _stop_owned_environments(self) -> None:
        for environment in list(self._session_environments.values()):
            environment.stop(force=True)
        environments = getattr(self._manager, "environments", {})
        for name, environment in list(environments.items()):
            if name == "wetlands_main":
                continue
            stop = getattr(environment, "stop", None)
            if callable(stop):
                stop(force=True)

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_spec(spec: EnvSpec) -> None:
        if not isinstance(spec, EnvSpec):
            raise TypeError("spec must be an EnvSpec")

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_workers(workers: int) -> None:
        if not isinstance(workers, int) or isinstance(workers, bool) or workers < 0:
            raise ValueError("workers must be a non-negative integer")

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_conflict_policy(on_conflict: str) -> None:
        if on_conflict not in _CONFLICT_POLICIES:
            raise ValueError("on_conflict must be one of 'error', 'reuse', 'update', or 'recreate'")

    @staticmethod
    def _validate_auto_start(auto_start: bool) -> None:
        if not isinstance(auto_start, bool):
            raise TypeError("auto_start must be a bool")

    def _registry_entry(self, name: str) -> dict[str, object] | None:
        payload = self._registry.load()
        envs = payload["envs"]
        if not isinstance(envs, dict):
            return None
        entry = envs.get(name)
        return dict(entry) if isinstance(entry, dict) else None

    def _remove_registry_entry(self, name: str) -> None:
        validate_environment_name(name)
        with root_lock(self.config.root):
            payload = self._registry.load()
            envs = payload["envs"]
            if isinstance(envs, dict):
                envs.pop(name, None)
            self._registry.save(payload)

    def _record_backend_failure(self, name: str, path: Path, spec: EnvSpec, error: Exception) -> None:
        self._registry.record_failure(name, path=path, spec=spec, error=str(error))

    def _create_owned(self, name: str, spec: EnvSpec, workers: int, auto_start: bool) -> tuple[Path, Ownership]:
        path = self._backend.environment_path(name)
        ownership: Ownership = "owned"
        try:
            path = self._backend.create(name, spec)
            if self._is_main_environment_path(path):
                ownership = "attached"
        except Exception as exc:
            self._record_backend_failure(name, path, spec, exc)
            raise
        self._registry.record_success(
            name,
            path=path,
            spec=spec,
            ownership=ownership,
            backend=self._backend.name,
            desired_workers=workers,
            auto_start=auto_start,
        )
        return path, ownership

    def _update_owned(self, name: str, spec: EnvSpec, path: Path, workers: int, auto_start: bool) -> Path:
        try:
            path = self._backend.update(name, spec)
        except Exception as exc:
            self._record_backend_failure(name, path, spec, exc)
            raise
        self._registry.record_success(
            name,
            path=path,
            spec=spec,
            ownership="owned",
            backend=self._backend.name,
            desired_workers=workers,
            auto_start=auto_start,
        )
        return path

    def _recreate_owned(self, name: str, spec: EnvSpec, path: Path, workers: int, auto_start: bool) -> Path:
        try:
            path = self._backend.recreate(name, spec)
        except Exception as exc:
            self._record_backend_failure(name, path, spec, exc)
            raise
        self._registry.record_success(
            name,
            path=path,
            spec=spec,
            ownership="owned",
            backend=self._backend.name,
            desired_workers=workers,
            auto_start=auto_start,
        )
        return path

    def _acquire_untracked(
        self,
        name: str,
        spec: EnvSpec,
        workers: int,
        on_conflict: ConflictPolicy,
        auto_start: bool,
    ) -> Path:
        path = self._backend.environment_path(name)
        if on_conflict == "error":
            raise EnvironmentConflictError(f"Environment '{name}' exists but is not tracked by Wetlands")
        if on_conflict == "reuse":
            ownership: Ownership = "accepted-untracked"
            self._registry.record_success(
                name,
                path=path,
                spec=spec,
                ownership=ownership,
                backend=self._backend.name,
                desired_workers=workers,
                auto_start=auto_start,
            )
            return path
        if on_conflict == "update":
            try:
                path = self._backend.update(name, spec)
            except Exception as exc:
                self._record_backend_failure(name, path, spec, exc)
                raise
            self._registry.record_success(
                name,
                path=path,
                spec=spec,
                ownership="accepted-untracked",
                backend=self._backend.name,
                desired_workers=workers,
                auto_start=auto_start,
            )
            return path
        return self._recreate_owned(name, spec, path, workers, auto_start)

    def _is_main_environment_path(self, path: Path) -> bool:
        is_main_environment_path = getattr(self._backend, "is_main_environment_path", None)
        return bool(callable(is_main_environment_path) and is_main_environment_path(path))

    def _validate_existing_entry_for_policy(self, name: str, path: Path) -> None:
        if self.config.main_env_policy != "only":
            return
        if self._is_main_environment_path(path):
            return
        raise EnvironmentConflictError(
            f"Environment '{name}' is not backed by the main environment required by main_env_policy='only'"
        )

    def _environment_handle(
        self,
        name: str,
        path: Path,
        spec: EnvSpec | None,
        ownership: Ownership,
        workers: int,
        auto_start: bool,
    ) -> _SessionEnvironment:
        environment = self._session_environments.get(name)
        if environment is None:
            environment = _SessionEnvironment(
                self,
                name=name,
                path=path,
                spec=spec,
                registry_owner=ownership,
                auto_start=auto_start,
            )
            self._session_environments[name] = environment
        else:
            environment.configure(path=path, spec=spec, registry_owner=ownership, auto_start=auto_start)
        previous_auto_start = environment.auto_start
        scale_kwargs = {"auto_start": auto_start} if auto_start is False or auto_start != previous_auto_start else {}
        environment.scale(workers, **scale_kwargs)
        return environment

class TaskStatus(enum.Enum):
    """Status of a task through its lifecycle."""

    PENDING = "pending"
    RUNNING = "running"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    FAILED = "failed"
    CANCELED = "canceled"

    def is_finished(self) -> bool:
        """Return True if the task has reached a terminal state."""
        return self in (TaskStatus.COMPLETED, TaskStatus.FAILED, TaskStatus.CANCELED)

class Task(Generic[T]):
    """Represents an asynchronous unit of work in a remote environment.

    Type parameter T is the return type of the remote function.
    """

    def __init__(self, task_id: str | None = None) -> None:
        self._id = task_id or str(uuid.uuid4())
        self._status = TaskStatus.PENDING
        self._result: T | None = None
        self._error: str | None = None
        self._traceback: list[str] | None = None
        self._exception: ExecutionException | None = None
        self._message: str | None = None
        self._current: int | None = None
        self._maximum: int | None = None
        self._outputs: dict[str, Any] = {}
        self._listeners: list[Callable[[TaskEvent], None]] = []
        self._terminal_event: TaskEvent | None = None
        self._future: Future[T] = Future()
        self._lock = threading.RLock()
        self._done_event = threading.Event()

        # Set by the environment before dispatch
        self._start_fn: Callable[[], None] | None = None
        self._cancel_fn: Callable[[], None] | None = None

    @property
    def id(self) -> str:
        return self._id

    @property
    def status(self) -> TaskStatus:
        return self._status

    @property
    def result(self) -> T:
        """The return value. Raises InvalidStateError if not COMPLETED."""
        if self._status != TaskStatus.COMPLETED:
            raise InvalidStateError(f"Task is {self._status.value}, not completed")
        return self._result  # type: ignore[return-value]

    @property
    def error(self) -> str | None:
        return self._error

    @property
    def traceback(self) -> list[str] | None:
        return self._traceback

    @property
    def exception(self) -> ExecutionException | None:
        return self._exception

    @property
    def message(self) -> str | None:
        return self._message

    @property
    def current(self) -> int | None:
        return self._current

    @property
    def maximum(self) -> int | None:
        return self._maximum

    @property
    def progress(self) -> float | None:
        """current / maximum as a float in [0, 1]. None if unavailable."""
        if self._current is not None and self._maximum is not None and self._maximum > 0:
            return self._current / self._maximum
        return None

    @property
    def outputs(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
        return self._outputs

    # --- Control ---

    def start(self) -> Self:
        """Dispatch the task to the remote environment.
        No-op if already started. Returns self for chaining.
        """
        with self._lock:
            if self._status != TaskStatus.PENDING:
                return self
            if self._start_fn is None:
                raise InvalidStateError("Task has no start function. Was it created via submit()?")
            start_fn = self._start_fn
        start_fn()
        return self

    def cancel(self) -> None:
        """Request cooperative cancellation.
        Sets a flag that the remote code can check via task.cancel_requested.
        Does nothing if the task is already finished.
        """
        with self._lock:
            if self._status.is_finished():
                return
            if self._cancel_fn is not None:
                self._cancel_fn()

    def wait_for(self, timeout: float | None = None) -> Self:
        """Block until the task reaches a terminal state.
        Raises TimeoutError if timeout (in seconds) is exceeded.
        Does NOT cancel the task on timeout (matches concurrent.futures behavior).
        Returns self for chaining.
        """
        if not self._done_event.wait(timeout=timeout):
            raise TimeoutError(f"Task {self._id} did not finish within {timeout}s")
        return self

    # --- Observation ---

    def listen(self, callback: Callable[[TaskEvent], None]) -> Self:
        """Register a listener for task events. Returns self for chaining.
        If the task has already reached a terminal state, the terminal event is
        replayed immediately to the callback.
        """
        with self._lock:
            self._listeners.append(callback)
            terminal = self._terminal_event
        if terminal is not None:
            callback(terminal)
        return self

    def wait(self, timeout: float | None = None) -> Self:
        """Wetlands 2 alias for wait_for(). Returns self."""
        return self.wait_for(timeout=timeout)

    def remove_listener(self, callback: Callable[[TaskEvent], None]) -> None:
        """Remove a previously registered listener."""
        with self._lock:
            self._listeners.remove(callback)

    # --- concurrent.futures interop ---

    @property
    def future(self) -> Future[T]:
        """A standard Future that resolves with the task result."""
        return self._future

    # --- Awaitable ---

    def __await__(self):
        """Makes Task awaitable: result = await task"""
        return self._async_result().__await__()

    async def _async_result(self) -> T:
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        result = await asyncio.wrap_future(self._future, loop=loop)
        return result

    # --- Async event stream ---

    async def events(self) -> AsyncIterator[TaskEvent]:
        """Async iterator over task events. Terminates after the terminal event."""
        queue: asyncio.Queue[TaskEvent | None] = asyncio.Queue()
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

        def _on_event(event: TaskEvent) -> None:
            loop.call_soon_threadsafe(queue.put_nowait, event)
            if event.type in (TaskEventType.COMPLETION, TaskEventType.FAILURE, TaskEventType.CANCELATION, TaskEventType.CANCELLATION):
                loop.call_soon_threadsafe(queue.put_nowait, None)

        self.listen(_on_event)
        while True:
            event = await queue.get()
            if event is None:
                break
            yield event

    # --- Context manager (auto-cancel on exit) ---

    def __enter__(self) -> Self:
        if self._status == TaskStatus.PENDING:
            self.start()
        return self

    def __exit__(self, exc_type: Any, exc_val: Any, exc_tb: Any) -> None:
        if not self._status.is_finished():
            self.cancel()
            self.wait_for()

    async def __aenter__(self) -> Self:
        if self._status == TaskStatus.PENDING:
            self.start()
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type: Any, exc_val: Any, exc_tb: Any) -> None:
        if not self._status.is_finished():
            self.cancel()
            await self

    # --- Internal methods (called by the environment/IPC reader) ---

    def _set_start_fn(self, fn: Callable[[], None]) -> None:
        self._start_fn = fn

    def _set_cancel_fn(self, fn: Callable[[], None]) -> None:
        self._cancel_fn = fn

    def _set_running(self) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            if self._status != TaskStatus.PENDING:
                return False
            self._status = TaskStatus.RUNNING
        self._emit(TaskEventType.STARTED)
        return True

    def _set_completed(self, result: T) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if self._status.is_finished():
                return
            self._status = TaskStatus.COMPLETED
            self._result = result
        self._future.set_result(result)
        self._emit(TaskEventType.COMPLETION)
        self._done_event.set()

    def _set_failed(self, error: str, traceback: list[str] | None = None) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if self._status.is_finished():
                return
            self._status = TaskStatus.FAILED
            self._error = error
            self._traceback = traceback
            self._exception = ExecutionException({"exception": error, "traceback": traceback or []})
        self._future.set_exception(self._exception)
        self._emit(TaskEventType.FAILURE)
        self._done_event.set()

    def _set_canceled(self) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if self._status.is_finished():
                return
            self._status = TaskStatus.CANCELED
        self._future.cancel()
        self._emit(TaskEventType.CANCELLATION)
        self._done_event.set()

    def _set_update(
        self,
        message: str | None = None,
        current: int | None = None,
        maximum: int | None = None,
        outputs: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if message is not None:
                self._message = message
            if current is not None:
                self._current = current
            if maximum is not None:
                self._maximum = maximum
            if outputs:
                self._outputs.update(outputs)
        self._emit(TaskEventType.UPDATE)

    def _emit(self, event_type: TaskEventType) -> None:
        event = TaskEvent(task=self, type=event_type)
        is_terminal = event_type in (TaskEventType.COMPLETION, TaskEventType.FAILURE, TaskEventType.CANCELATION, TaskEventType.CANCELLATION)
        with self._lock:
            if is_terminal:
                self._terminal_event = event
            listeners = list(self._listeners)
        for listener in listeners:
            listener(event)

    def _on_message(self, message: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
        """Handle an IPC message from the remote worker."""
        action = message.get("action")
        if action == "execution finished":
            self._set_completed(message.get("result"))
        elif action == "error":
            self._set_failed(message.get("exception", "Unknown error"), message.get("traceback"))
        elif action == "update":
            self._set_update(
                message=message.get("message"),
                current=message.get("current"),
                maximum=message.get("maximum"),
                outputs=message.get("outputs"),
            )
        elif action == "canceled":
            self._set_canceled()

    """Request cooperative cancellation.
    Sets a flag that the remote code can check via task.cancel_requested.
    Does nothing if the task is already finished.
    """
    with self._lock:
        if self._status.is_finished():
            return
        if self._cancel_fn is not None:
            self._cancel_fn()

def wait_for(self, timeout: float | None = None) -> Self:
    """Block until the task reaches a terminal state.
    Raises TimeoutError if timeout (in seconds) is exceeded.
    Does NOT cancel the task on timeout (matches concurrent.futures behavior).
    Returns self for chaining.
    """
    if not self._done_event.wait(timeout=timeout):
        raise TimeoutError(f"Task {self._id} did not finish within {timeout}s")
    return self

def listen(self, callback: Callable[[TaskEvent], None]) -> Self:
    """Register a listener for task events. Returns self for chaining.
    If the task has already reached a terminal state, the terminal event is
    replayed immediately to the callback.
    """
    with self._lock:
        self._listeners.append(callback)
        terminal = self._terminal_event
    if terminal is not None:
        callback(terminal)
    return self

async def events(self) -> AsyncIterator[TaskEvent]:
    """Async iterator over task events. Terminates after the terminal event."""
    queue: asyncio.Queue[TaskEvent | None] = asyncio.Queue()
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

    def _on_event(event: TaskEvent) -> None:
        loop.call_soon_threadsafe(queue.put_nowait, event)
        if event.type in (TaskEventType.COMPLETION, TaskEventType.FAILURE, TaskEventType.CANCELATION, TaskEventType.CANCELLATION):
            loop.call_soon_threadsafe(queue.put_nowait, None)

    self.listen(_on_event)
    while True:
        event = await queue.get()
        if event is None:
            break
        yield event

class RemoteTaskHandle:
    """Available to remote code for progress reporting and cancellation.

    Injected by the module_executor when the function signature has a 'task' parameter.
    """

    def __init__(self, task_id: str, connection_lock: threading.Lock, connection: Any) -> None:
        self._task_id = task_id
        self._cancel_requested = False
        self._lock = connection_lock
        self._connection = connection

    @property
    def cancel_requested(self) -> bool:
        """True if the caller has requested cancellation."""
        return self._cancel_requested

    def update(
        self,
        message: str | None = None,
        *,
        current: int | None = None,
        maximum: int | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        """Report progress. Sends an UPDATE event to the caller."""
        payload: dict[str, Any] = {"action": "update", "task_id": self._task_id}
        if message is not None:
            payload["message"] = message
        if current is not None:
            payload["current"] = current
        if maximum is not None:
            payload["maximum"] = maximum
        with self._lock:
            self._connection.send(payload)

    def set_output(self, key: str, value: Any) -> None:
        """Publish a named intermediate output (must be picklable)."""
        payload = {
            "action": "update",
            "task_id": self._task_id,
            "outputs": {key: value},
        }
        with self._lock:
            self._connection.send(payload)

    def cancel(self) -> None:
        """Acknowledge cancellation. Transitions the task to CANCELED."""
        payload = {"action": "canceled", "task_id": self._task_id}
        with self._lock:
            self._connection.send(payload)

    def log(self, message: str, level: int = logging.INFO) -> None:
        """Send a log message to the caller's logging system."""
        payload = {
            "action": "log",
            "task_id": self._task_id,
            "message": message,
            "level": level,
        }
        with self._lock:
            self._connection.send(payload)

    def _set_cancel_requested(self) -> None:
        """Called by the module_executor when a cancel message is received."""
        self._cancel_requested = True
